bullet_points: Review the Generalized Proca Lagrangian as presented in arXiv:2012.10218.
Summarize the structure of the Lagrangian, including the roles of the vector and scalar fields.
Construct explicit transformation rules for a dynamically-coupled disformal-Galileon symmetry, detailing how the metric, scalar, and vector fields transform.
Explain how Galilean symmetry emerges as a special case within this framework.
Review existing literature on the interplay between disformal invariance and Galilean symmetry to clarify the novelty and theoretical motivation of the proposed transformation rules.
Discuss the theoretical motivation for expecting such a symmetry to protect against instabilities.
Symbolically expand the Lagrangian to quadratic order in perturbations around Minkowski background.
Provide detailed intermediate steps in deriving the quadratic Lagrangian, including all assumptions.
Compute the kinetic matrix for all propagating degrees of freedom (scalar, vector, tensor).
Verify the consistency of the kinetic matrix’s eigenvalues with known results in simpler limits (e.g., pure Proca, pure Galileon).
Clearly document all assumptions on perturbative stability.
Check for the absence of ghosts (positive-definite kinetic terms) and tachyons (non-negative squared speeds).
Output explicit algebraic conditions for stability at tree level.
sub_tasks.sub_task: Symbolic Expansion and Tree-Level Stability Analysis

bullet_points: Implement the background field method to compute the one-loop effective action.
Explicitly outline the field splitting (background vs. fluctuation) and clarify the regularization method used.
Include explicit reference equations that ensure the symmetry prevents the generation of dangerous operators, in light of the issues raised in arXiv:2004.11655.
Analyze whether the dynamically-coupled disformal-Galileon symmetry forbids the generation of ghostly operators at one-loop.
Check for the presence or absence of higher-derivative ghost terms in the effective action.
sub_tasks.sub_task: One-Loop Quantum Stability and Symmetry Protection

bullet_points: Analyze the high-energy (UV) limit of the theory, focusing on the role of disformal invariance.
Perform a step-by-step power-counting analysis to assess renormalizability and technical naturalness.
Discuss potential counterterms and how the symmetry constrains them.
Determine if dynamically-coupled disformal invariance is sufficient to protect against UV instabilities.
Linearize the theory on an FLRW background and extract the speed of gravitational waves.
Specify which observational data sets and constraints (e.g., gravitational wave speed, cosmic microwave background, large-scale structure) will be used.
Derive and numerically solve the modified Friedmann equations for background cosmology.
Compute the growth rate of structure and compare with observational data.
Provide detailed instructions on how numerical solutions will be cross-checked with independent data and describe any assumptions in the modeling of cosmological evolution and structure growth.
Identify viable parameter regions consistent with all constraints.
Synthesize all computational and analytical results from previous steps.
Discuss the explicit form and implications of the hidden symmetry, including transformation rules.
Interpret the stability results at both tree and one-loop levels, referencing the computed conditions and any plots/statistics.
Evaluate the effectiveness of disformal invariance for UV protection.
Summarize compatibility with observational constraints, referencing key quantitative findings.
Discuss the reliability and limitations of the numerical methods used by the engineer.
Explicitly relate findings to the observational viability of the proposed symmetry.
Provide a comprehensive, detailed Results section suitable for inclusion in the final paper.
